museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A quieter month in the boom: the Spectrum is newly real, arcades keep cycling hits, and the year's bigger hardware shocks are still just over the horizon.
museum.curatorNote: May has fewer clean release anchors than March, April, August or September. This draft treats it as a post-Spectrum, pre-ColecoVision context month and marks broad items accordingly.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: The new UK micro era begins to settle into ordinary routines.

items.title: The ZX Spectrum becomes a real UK ownership question
items.body: After April's launch, the Spectrum moved from announcement to availability, reviews, adverts and waiting lists.
items.sourceNote: Spectrum launched 23 April 1982; May is early ownership and retail context.

items.title: Cassette software starts to feel like a market
items.body: Small UK software firms, mail-order adverts and typed listings made games feel close to readers rather than distant corporations.

items.title: Arcade cabinets continue to set the visual standard
items.body: Dig Dug, Ms. Pac-Man, Zaxxon and Robotron represented four different answers to what a modern arcade game could look and feel like.

items.title: Atari Pac-Man becomes a talking point
items.body: The Atari 2600 port had already sold massively, but its compromises were becoming part of home-game discussion.
items.sourceNote: Atari 2600 Pac-Man released in March with April promotion.

items.title: The American console race widens offstage
items.body: ColecoVision was not yet a mass shelf object, but the idea of better arcade-style home conversions was gathering force before its summer rollout.
items.sourceNote: ColecoVision rolled out from late July/August 1982.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: May is a shelf-context month rather than a clean launch calendar.

items.title: ZX Spectrum early software
items.body: The first wave of Spectrum gaming was small, strange and often cassette-based.
items.sourceNote: Platform launched late April; individual May title dates not asserted.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: Fresh internationally, the game remained an arcade presence through spring.

items.title: Robotron: 2084
items.body: The twin-stick Williams cabinet continued its move from showpiece to player legend.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: By May, it was a defining sequel rather than a novelty.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Still the cabinet that made players tilt their heads before touching the controls.

items.title: Pac-Man
items.body: A giant home release whose reputation was already complicated.

items.title: BBC Micro games
items.body: The BBC Micro was becoming a place for education and play to overlap uneasily.

items.title: VIC-20 games
items.body: Commodore's pre-C64 colour micro kept a place in UK homes and adverts.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The home computer starts to look ordinary enough to be radical.

items.title: ZX Spectrum after launch
items.body: Its importance was not just specification; it was the price at which programming and games entered British homes.

items.title: Atari 2600 installed base
items.body: The 2600 was everywhere enough that one cartridge could become a national talking point.

items.title: Arcade hardware variety
items.body: Cabinets had custom controls, unique boards and public presence home systems could not match.

items.title: Mail-order micro economy
items.body: Hardware, tapes and peripherals circulated through adverts as much as through shops.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The magazine shelf was now also a software shelf.

items.title: Spectrum early coverage
items.headline: The new Sinclair machine settles in
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for May 1982.
items.curatorNote: Weekly coverage helped turn launch curiosity into routines: adverts, tips, listings, prices.
items.body: Weekly coverage helped turn launch curiosity into routines: adverts, tips, listings, prices.

items.title: Home-computer seriousness
items.headline: The practical home machine
items.curatorNote: PCW made the machines respectable even when readers wanted games.
items.body: PCW made the machines respectable even when readers wanted games.

items.title: C&VG hybrid shelf
items.headline: Arcade hits and micro listings
items.curatorNote: The hybrid title captured the moment: computer and video games were not yet the same thing, but they were sharing pages.
items.body: The hybrid title captured the moment: computer and video games were not yet the same thing, but they were sharing pages.

items.title: Electronic Games boom lens
items.headline: The arcade market as national culture
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for May 1982.
items.curatorNote: American games-magazine confidence still matched the boom's public mood.
items.body: American games-magazine confidence still matched the boom's public mood.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: For a UK home player, the network was a magazine and a postbox.

items.title: Listings made readers into operators
items.body: Typing a game changed the reader from consumer to participant.

items.title: Adverts were discovery
items.body: A small classified could be the first sight of a new game.

items.title: Arcade gossip was physical
items.body: Cabinet knowledge moved through queues and over shoulders.

items.title: Remote computing stayed niche
items.body: Modems existed, but not as everyday gaming infrastructure.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: May felt like the month after a machine enters the house.

items.title: The Spectrum made games feel local
items.body: A game could come from a shop, a magazine or someone nearby.

items.title: The arcade still won on spectacle
items.body: No cassette loading screen looked like a cabinet marquee.

items.title: The market felt crowded in a good way
items.body: More machines meant more possibilities, not yet more confusion.

items.title: Everything required patience
items.body: Saving, loading, ordering and learning all took time.

sources.label: ColecoVision release context
sources.note: Used for pre-launch summer rollout context.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft; needs human editorial review and source verification.
